[id 130] Got the inline tool inventory. Available inline commands the DSL needs to support: `edit` (`<replace>`), `write`, `delete_file` (`<delete>`), `rename_file` (`<rename>`), `commit`, `check`, `run_tests`, `think`. The DSL only needs to cover the common ones for ergonomic test-writing — anything exotic can fall back to raw inline XML.

Before I write anything, let me think about the harness's relationship to `LiveSession` carefully.

`LiveSession` runs through `_process_llm_request` → backend.stream() → `_on_stream_finished` → either inline-pipeline branch or finish-stream branch. With `SyncTaskRunner` the whole graph collapses to a straight call chain, so when `harness.user_says("hi")` returns, the session has already done everything it can do up to the next "I need an LLM response" boundary.

But: `send_message()` goes RUNNING → `_process_llm_request()` → consumes one queued backend response → emits chunks → `_on_stream_finished` → maybe runs inline commands → if there were tool_calls, `_execute_tool_calls()` → after all tools finish, `_continue_after_tools()` → calls `_process_llm_request()` AGAIN → wants another backend response. So the harness needs ScriptedBackend pre-loaded with however many responses it'll take to finish the turn, OR we accept that `ai_says()` queues exactly one and "the turn drains until it runs out of queued responses or finishes."

For Phase 4's scope, I'll do the simpler thing: `ai_says(dsl)` queues exactly one scripted response, and `user_says(text)` triggers the session and runs synchronously. If the session asks for another response, the test fails with "scripted backend exhausted" — which is the right behavior for the migrated tests (both are single-turn). Multi-turn tests would queue multiple `ai_says()` calls before `user_says()` (or call `ai_says()` then have the harness auto-trigger the next iteration via `_continue_after_tools`).

Wait — that doesn't quite work either. For multi-turn, you want:
```
session.user_says("...")          # triggers turn, drains LLM
session.ai_says(...)              # queue response 1
# but user_says already triggered, scripted backend was empty
```

The right ordering is **queue THEN trigger**:
```
session.expects_ai(dsl1)
session.expects_ai(dsl2)
session.user_says("...")          # now triggers, drains both responses
```

Or, equivalently and more readable: `ai_says()` is a **planning** call (queues the response), not an action. The test reads as a script: "user will say X, AI will respond with Y, then we check stuff." Trigger happens at the moment we run assertions, OR at an explicit `.run()` call, OR — best — `user_says()` is the trigger and `ai_says()` only queues.

I'll go with that: `ai_says()` queues, `user_says()` triggers. For the two migrated tests this is fine; both are single-turn and "user_says first, then ai_says, then assertions" reads naturally if we make `ai_says()` either-order-friendly. Easiest: `user_says()` doesn't trigger by itself; there's an explicit `.run_turn()` (or `.run()`) that starts processing. That keeps ordering free and makes the trigger point explicit.

Final API:
```python
session.given_files({...})
session.given_files_in_context("a.py", "b.py")
session.given_failing_tests()
session.user_says("Fix both...")
session.ai_says("""@edit a.py ...""")
session.run_turn()   # triggers, drains synchronously
assert session.last_turn.failed_at == "run_tests"
assert session.vfs["a.py"] == "..."
```

Now — building a real `LiveSession` requires building a real `SessionManager`, which requires a real `ForgeRepository`, which requires `pygit2.init_repository`. The existing fixtures already do this. I'll factor it into `tests/harness/repo.py`.

`SessionManager.__init__` also calls `start_summary_generation()` which kicks off background work. With `SyncTaskRunner` this would actually try to call `LLMClient(api_key, model).chat()` synchronously and hit the network. Need to disable. Two options: (a) inject `SyncTaskRunner` AND a no-op summary path, (b) monkeypatch `start_summary_generation` to no-op like the existing test fixture does. Going with (b) — minimal, matches existing fixture behavior.

OK, building. Note: `LiveSession` always calls `cancel_all()` from `_cleanup_threads`, which on `SyncTaskRunner` is fine. And `vfs.claim_thread()` / `release_thread()` need to exist on the VFS; they do.

One more wrinkle: when `_process_llm_request` runs synchronously, after `start_streaming()` it calls `self._tasks.submit(stream_work, ...)`. With SyncTaskRunner this runs `stream_work` immediately, which calls `backend.stream()` which yields events through `emit`, calls `on_event` (which emits Qt signals via `_emit_event`), and finally calls `on_result` → `_on_stream_finished` → which may recursively call `_process_llm_request` again. As long as ScriptedBackend has another response queued, this works fine — Python's recursion can handle a few levels.

Let me get on with it.

<write file="tests/harness/__init__.py">
"""
Session test harness for Forge.

A minimal-ceremony way to write flow tests for the AI session pipeline.
Tests look like a script:

    def test_failed_run_tests_preserves_edits(session):
        session.given_files({
            "a.py": "def foo():\\n    return 1\\n",
            "b.py": "def bar():\\n    return 10\\n",
        })
        session.given_files_in_context("a.py", "b.py")
        session.given_failing_tests()

        session.user_says("Fix foo->2 and bar->20, then run tests.")
        session.ai_says('''
            I'll fix both.
            @edit a.py
                old: return 1
                new: return 2
            @edit b.py
                old: return 10
                new: return 20
            @run_tests
        ''')
        session.run_turn()

        assert session.last_turn.failed_at == "run_tests"
        assert session.vfs["a.py"] == "def foo():\\n    return 2\\n"
        assert session.vfs["b.py"] == "def bar():\\n    return 20\\n"

The harness sits on top of three Phase-1/2/3 seams:

  - SyncTaskRunner so the pipeline executes straight-line.
  - ScriptedBackend so no network is touched.
  - The extracted runtime helpers (stream_to_events, run_inline_commands,
    execute_tool_calls), which are what make the whole pipeline observable
    deterministically.

API surface (see SessionTestHarness for full docs):

  Setup (chainable, return self):
    .given_files({path: content, ...})
    .given_files_in_context(*paths)
    .given_passing_tests() / .given_failing_tests()

  Script (queues, doesn't trigger):
    .user_says(text)
    .ai_says(dsl_or_raw)        - DSL gets compiled to inline XML
    .ai_says_raw(content)       - skip DSL compilation
    .ai_returns_tool_calls([...])  - rare; queue an API tool-call response

  Run:
    .run_turn()                 - drains the queued script

  Inspect:
    .vfs[path]                  - current VFS state
    .last_turn                  - TurnResult from the most recent .run_turn()
    .messages                   - LiveSession.messages
    .next_prompt_text()         - rendered user-side text from to_messages()
    .prompt_blocks              - PromptManager block list

Use the `session` fixture from `tests/conftest.py` to get a configured
SessionTestHarness.
"""

from tests.harness.dsl import compile_dsl
from tests.harness.session import SessionTestHarness, TurnResult

__all__ = ["SessionTestHarness", "TurnResult", "compile_dsl"]
</write>

<write file="tests/harness/repo.py">
"""
Git repository bootstrap helpers for the test harness.

Extracted from the duplicated fixtures in tests/test_branch_workspace_vfs.py
and tests/test_inline_pipeline_vfs.py. Both did the same dance: init a
pygit2 repo, write a tree of seed files, create one commit on master.
"""

from pathlib import Path

import pygit2

from forge.git_backend.repository import ForgeRepository

_DEFAULT_SIG = pygit2.Signature("Test", "test@test.com")


def bootstrap_repo(
    tmp_path: Path,
    files: dict[str, str | bytes] | None = None,
    branch: str = "master",
) -> ForgeRepository:
    """Initialize a git repo at tmp_path with one commit holding `files`.

    Args:
        tmp_path: directory to init the repo in (typically the pytest tmp_path).
        files: path -> content mapping for the seed commit. Empty/None creates
            a repo with a single placeholder file (`.gitkeep`) so the initial
            commit isn't empty (some tooling chokes on empty trees).
        branch: branch name to create the commit on. Defaults to "master" to
            match the existing test suite's convention.

    Returns:
        A ForgeRepository pointing at the new repo, with HEAD on `branch`.
    """
    pygit2.init_repository(str(tmp_path))
    raw = pygit2.Repository(str(tmp_path))

    if not files:
        files = {".gitkeep": ""}

    tb = raw.TreeBuilder()
    for name, data in files.items():
        if isinstance(data, str):
            data = data.encode()
        blob = raw.create_blob(data)
        tb.insert(name, blob, pygit2.GIT_FILEMODE_BLOB)
    tree = tb.write()
    raw.create_commit(
        f"refs/heads/{branch}", _DEFAULT_SIG, _DEFAULT_SIG, "initial", tree, []
    )
    raw.set_head(f"refs/heads/{branch}")

    return ForgeRepository(str(tmp_path))


# Hermetic Makefile bodies for given_passing_tests / given_failing_tests.
# Both use printf + true/false so they don't depend on bash being available
# and don't touch any files (writeback after the test command is therefore
# always a no-op, which is what we want for deterministic flow tests).
PASSING_MAKEFILE = "test:\n\t@printf 'simulated test pass\\n'; true\n"
FAILING_MAKEFILE = "test:\n\t@printf 'simulated test failure\\n'; false\n"
</write>

Now the DSL compiler. The DSL needs to map directives to inline XML the existing parser understands. The complication is `@edit ... old: ... new: ...` — this needs indentation parsing. I'll keep it tight.

<write file="tests/harness/dsl.py">
"""
DSL compiler: terse test-friendly syntax → inline XML the parser understands.

Why a DSL? Because writing inline-command XML directly inside a Python
test means literal `<replace>`/`</old>`/`</new>` tags inside triple-quoted
strings, which is awkward to read AND occasionally trips up tooling that
scans the source. The DSL keeps tests skimmable.

Supported directives (everything else passes through as prose):

    @edit <path>
        old: <text...>
        new: <text...>

    @write <path>
        <full file content>

    @delete <path>

    @rename <old_path> -> <new_path>

    @run_tests
    @check
    @commit message=<text>
    @think
        <conclusion text>

Indentation rules (whitespace-significant, like YAML or Python):

  - A directive line starts with `@` at any column.
  - Subsequent lines that are MORE INDENTED than the directive are part
    of the directive's body. Once we see a line that is at-or-less-
    indented than the directive (and isn't blank), the body ends.
  - Inside an `@edit` body, the labels `old:` and `new:` start their
    respective sections; their content runs until the next label, the
    next `@` directive, or end-of-body.
  - Blank lines are kept as blank lines; they do NOT end a body.

Anything outside a directive is emitted verbatim as prose, so you can
mix narration with commands the way an LLM actually would.

Escape hatch: write inline XML directly. The compiler only consumes
lines beginning with `@` (after optional leading whitespace) — raw XML
is just prose to it.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import re
import textwrap
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class _Directive:
    name: str  # e.g. "edit", "run_tests"
    head: str  # text on the directive line after the name (e.g. "a.py")
    body_lines: list[str]  # de-indented body lines (no trailing newline each)
    indent: int  # column where the `@` started


def compile_dsl(text: str) -> str:
    """Compile DSL `text` into the assistant content format (prose + inline XML).

    Pure function. The output is what an LLM "would have said" — you can
    feed it straight into ScriptedBackend.queue_response(content=...) or
    pass it through parse_inline_commands directly.
    """
    text = textwrap.dedent(text).strip("\n")
    lines = text.split("\n")

    out_parts: list[str] = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]
        stripped = line.lstrip()

        if stripped.startswith("@"):
            directive, consumed = _parse_directive(lines, i)
            out_parts.append(_render_directive(directive))
            i += consumed
        else:
            # Plain prose line — emit verbatim.
            out_parts.append(line)
            i += 1

    # Strip leading/trailing blank lines from the joined output but keep
    # internal structure.
    return "\n".join(out_parts).strip("\n") + "\n"


# --- Parsing ---------------------------------------------------------


_DIRECTIVE_RE = re.compile(r"^(\s*)@(\w+)(.*)$")


def _parse_directive(lines: list[str], start: int) -> tuple[_Directive, int]:
    """Parse a directive at lines[start]. Returns (directive, lines_consumed)."""
    m = _DIRECTIVE_RE.match(lines[start])
    if m is None:  # pragma: no cover — caller already checked
        raise ValueError(f"not a directive: {lines[start]!r}")
    indent_str, name, head = m.groups()
    indent = len(indent_str)
    head = head.strip()

    # Collect body lines: those more indented than the directive (or blank).
    body_lines: list[str] = []
    j = start + 1
    while j < len(lines):
        candidate = lines[j]
        if candidate.strip() == "":
            body_lines.append("")
            j += 1
            continue
        # Stop if we're back at-or-less indented than the directive.
        leading = len(candidate) - len(candidate.lstrip())
        if leading <= indent:
            break
        body_lines.append(candidate)
        j += 1

    # De-indent the body to the minimum indent of non-blank lines.
    if body_lines:
        body_lines = _dedent_block(body_lines)

    return _Directive(name=name, head=head, body_lines=body_lines, indent=indent), j - start


def _dedent_block(lines: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """De-indent a block to its smallest non-blank indent."""
    min_indent = min(
        (len(line) - len(line.lstrip()) for line in lines if line.strip()),
        default=0,
    )
    return [line[min_indent:] if line.strip() else "" for line in lines]


# --- Rendering -------------------------------------------------------


def _render_directive(d: _Directive) -> str:
    handler = _RENDERERS.get(d.name)
    if handler is None:
        raise ValueError(
            f"unknown DSL directive: @{d.name}. "
            f"Known: {sorted(_RENDERERS)}. "
            f"For unsupported tools, write inline XML directly."
        )
    return handler(d)


def _render_edit(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@edit <path> with `old:` / `new:` sub-blocks."""
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@edit requires a file path")

    sections = _split_labeled_sections(d.body_lines, ("old:", "new:"))
    if "old" not in sections or "new" not in sections:
        raise ValueError(
            f"@edit {path}: requires both `old:` and `new:` sub-blocks; "
            f"got {sorted(sections)}"
        )

    old_text = "\n".join(sections["old"]).rstrip("\n")
    new_text = "\n".join(sections["new"]).rstrip("\n")

    return (
        f'<replace file="{path}">\n'
        f"<old>\n{old_text}\n</old>\n"
        f"<new>\n{new_text}\n</new>\n"
        f"</replace>"
    )


def _render_write(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@write <path> with raw body."""
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@write requires a file path")
    body = "\n".join(d.body_lines).rstrip("\n")
    return f'<write file="{path}">\n{body}\n</write>'


def _render_delete(d: _Directive) -> str:
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@delete requires a file path")
    return f'<delete file="{path}"/>

[INLINE COMMAND ERROR: File not found: {path}]
'


def _render_rename(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@rename old_path -> new_path"""
    parts = [p.strip() for p in d.head.split("->")]
    if len(parts) != 2 or not parts[0] or not parts[1]:
        raise ValueError(f"@rename requires `old_path -> new_path`; got {d.head!r}")
    return f'<rename old="{parts[0]}" new="{parts[1]}"/>'


def _render_run_tests(d: _Directive) -> str:
    # @run_tests can take optional attrs in the head; pass through verbatim.
    return f"<run_tests{(' ' + d.head) if d.head else ''}/>"


def _render_check(d: _Directive) -> str:
    return "<check/>"


def _render_commit(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@commit message=<text>  OR  @commit  (auto-generated message)."""
    head = d.head.strip()
    if head.startswith("message="):
        msg = head[len("message=") :].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        return f'<commit message="{msg}"/>'
    if not head:
        return '<commit message="test commit"/>'
    raise ValueError(f"@commit head not understood: {head!r}")


def _render_think(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@think with body becomes a <conclusion> inline tag."""
    body = "\n".join(d.body_lines).strip()
    return f"<conclusion>{body}</conclusion>"


_RENDERERS = {
    "edit": _render_edit,
    "write": _render_write,
    "delete": _render_delete,
    "rename": _render_rename,
    "run_tests": _render_run_tests,
    "check": _render_check,
    "commit": _render_commit,
    "think": _render_think,
}


# --- Helpers ---------------------------------------------------------


def _split_labeled_sections(
    lines: list[str], labels: tuple[str, ...]
) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    """Split `lines` into sections introduced by `label:` markers.

    A label line like `old:` (optionally followed by inline content on the
    same line) starts a new section. Subsequent lines belong to that
    section until the next label or end-of-body.

    Returns a dict mapping label name (without trailing `:`) to its list
    of content lines.
    """
    label_set = {lbl.rstrip(":") for lbl in labels}
    sections: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
    current: str | None = None

    for line in lines:
        stripped = line.strip()
        # Detect "label:" or "label: inline content"
        if ":" in stripped:
            head, _, rest = stripped.partition(":")
            if head in label_set and (rest == "" or rest.startswith(" ") or rest != ""):
                current = head
                sections[current] = []
                if rest.strip():
                    # Strip the single space after the colon to keep content tidy.
                    sections[current].append(rest.lstrip(" "))
                continue
        if current is not None:
            sections[current].append(line)

    return sections
</write>

Now the main harness class. It needs to build a real `SessionManager` + `LiveSession` with `SyncTaskRunner` + `ScriptedBackend` injected, and provide all the assertion lenses.

<write file="tests/harness/session.py">
"""
SessionTestHarness — the actual fixture object that tests interact with.

Wires up:
  - real ForgeRepository (via tests.harness.repo.bootstrap_repo)
  - real SessionManager + LiveSession
  - SyncTaskRunner so all off-thread work runs straight-line
  - ScriptedBackend so no LLM network call ever happens

Provides:
  - given_*  setup methods (chainable)
  - user_says / ai_says script methods (queue, do not trigger)
  - run_turn  trigger
  - vfs / messages / next_prompt_text / last_turn  inspection
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from forge.config.settings import Settings
from forge.git_backend.repository import ForgeRepository
from forge.runtime import ScriptedBackend, SyncTaskRunner
from forge.session.live_session import LiveSession, SessionState
from forge.session.manager import SessionManager
from tests.harness.dsl import compile_dsl
from tests.harness.repo import (
    FAILING_MAKEFILE,
    PASSING_MAKEFILE,
    bootstrap_repo,
)


@dataclass
class TurnResult:
    """Snapshot of what happened during the most recent run_turn().

    Built by inspecting LiveSession state immediately after the
    synchronous turn completes. The harness always rebuilds this from
    scratch each turn — there's no accumulation across turns.
    """

    succeeded: bool
    failed_at: str | None  # tool_name of the first failed inline command, if any
    inline_results: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    final_state: str = SessionState.IDLE
    error_message: str | None = None


class SessionTestHarness:
    """Fixture object exposing a fluent API for session flow tests.

    Construct with `SessionTestHarness(tmp_path)`. The repo is initialized
    immediately (so .vfs is available right away); the LiveSession is
    constructed lazily on the first method call that needs it (i.e. when
    you start scripting or triggering work). This keeps `given_files`
    cheap and defers the SessionManager bootstrap until necessary.
    """

    DEFAULT_BRANCH = "master"

    def __init__(self, tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: Any) -> None:
        self._tmp_path = tmp_path
        self._monkeypatch = monkeypatch

        # Disable background summary generation BEFORE constructing
        # SessionManager — otherwise it kicks off via the task runner on
        # __init__ and (with SyncTaskRunner) would hit the network
        # synchronously trying to call the summarization model.
        self._monkeypatch.setattr(
            "forge.session.manager.SessionManager.start_summary_generation",
            lambda self, force_refresh=False: None,
        )

        self._repo: ForgeRepository = bootstrap_repo(tmp_path, branch=self.DEFAULT_BRANCH)
        self._settings = self._make_settings()

        self._task_runner = SyncTaskRunner()
        self._backend = ScriptedBackend()

        self._session_manager: SessionManager | None = None
        self._session: LiveSession | None = None
        self._last_turn: TurnResult | None = None

    # --- Internal construction --------------------------------------

    def _make_settings(self) -> Settings:
        """Build a minimal Settings without touching disk."""
        s = Settings.__new__(Settings)
        s.config = {}
        return s

    def _ensure_session(self) -> LiveSession:
        """Lazily construct SessionManager + LiveSession on first use."""
        if self._session is not None:
            return self._session

        self._session_manager = SessionManager(
            repo=self._repo,
            branch_name=self.DEFAULT_BRANCH,
            settings=self._settings,
            task_runner=self._task_runner,
        )
        self._session = LiveSession(
            session_manager=self._session_manager,
            task_runner=self._task_runner,
            llm_backend=self._backend,
        )
        return self._session

    # --- Setup (chainable) ------------------------------------------

    def given_files(self, files: dict[str, str]) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Seed files into the repo BEFORE the session is constructed.

        Files are committed to the initial tree on the master branch.
        Only call this before any other action — once the session is
        live, use VFS writes (or DSL `@write`) instead.
        """
        if self._session is not None:
            raise RuntimeError(
                "given_files() must be called before any session "
                "interaction (user_says, ai_says, run_turn, vfs access)."
            )
        # Re-bootstrap the repo with the new file set (cheap on tmp_path).
        # Replace the existing tmp_path contents.
        for child in self._tmp_path.iterdir():
            if child.is_dir():
                _rmtree(child)
            else:
                child.unlink()
        self._repo = bootstrap_repo(
            self._tmp_path, files=files, branch=self.DEFAULT_BRANCH
        )
        return self

    def given_files_in_context(self, *paths: str) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Mark files as active context (so their content appears in the prompt)."""
        sm = self._ensure_session().session_manager
        for path in paths:
            sm.add_active_file(path)
        return self

    def given_passing_tests(self) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Install a hermetic Makefile whose `make test` exits 0."""
        return self._install_makefile(PASSING_MAKEFILE)

    def given_failing_tests(self) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Install a hermetic Makefile whose `make test` exits non-zero."""
        return self._install_makefile(FAILING_MAKEFILE)

    def _install_makefile(self, body: str) -> SessionTestHarness:
        # Write directly to the repo tree so it's part of the base state,
        # not a pending VFS change. Easiest: write to disk and re-commit.
        # Even easier: write through the VFS as a pending change. Both
        # work because run_tests materializes pending changes to a tempdir.
        # We use the VFS path so given_*_tests() works after session init.
        vfs = self._ensure_session().session_manager.vfs
        vfs.write_file("Makefile", body)
        return self

    # --- Script (queue, no trigger) ---------------------------------

    def user_says(self, text: str) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Queue a user message for the next run_turn().

        Stored on the harness; consumed when run_turn() is called.
        """
        self._ensure_session()
        self._pending_user_message = text
        return self

    def ai_says(self, dsl_text: str) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Queue an AI response, compiled from DSL into inline XML."""
        return self.ai_says_raw(compile_dsl(dsl_text))

    def ai_says_raw(self, content: str) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Queue an AI response with raw assistant content (no DSL compilation)."""
        self._ensure_session()
        self._backend.queue_response(content=content)
        return self

    def ai_returns_tool_calls(
        self, tool_calls: list[dict[str, Any]], content: str = ""
    ) -> SessionTestHarness:
        """Queue an AI response that issues API-style tool calls."""
        self._ensure_session()
        self._backend.queue_response(content=content, tool_calls=tool_calls)
        return self

    # --- Trigger ----------------------------------------------------

    def run_turn(self) -> TurnResult:
        """Send the queued user message and drain the session synchronously.

        Returns a TurnResult; also stored as `self.last_turn`.
        Because we use SyncTaskRunner, when send_message returns the
        session has either reached a terminal state (IDLE, COMPLETED,
        ERROR, WAITING_*) or has exhausted its scripted backend.
        """
        session = self._ensure_session()

        text = getattr(self, "_pending_user_message", "")
        self._pending_user_message = ""

        # send_message synchronously kicks off _process_llm_request which,
        # with SyncTaskRunner, runs the entire pipeline inline.
        session.send_message(text, _skip_workdir_check=True)

        self._last_turn = self._build_turn_result(session)
        return self._last_turn

    def _build_turn_result(self, session: LiveSession) -> TurnResult:
        """Inspect session state after a turn and synthesize a TurnResult."""
        # Look at the most recent assistant message for inline-results
        # metadata that _on_inline_commands_finished stashed there.
        inline_results: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
        for msg in reversed(session.messages):
            if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                inline_results = msg.get("_inline_results", []) or []
                break

        failed_at: str | None = None
        error_message: str | None = None
        # An inline result has shape {"success": bool, ...}. Walk the
        # results in order; the first failure (if any) is the failure point.
        # The tool_name lives on the parsed command, but we don't have the
        # command list here — fish it out of the result if present, else
        # rely on a sentinel error message below.
        for res in inline_results:
            if not res.get("success", True):
                # Some tools include "tool" in their result; if not, fall
                # back to scanning the synthesized error in messages.
                failed_at = res.get("tool") or _extract_failed_tool_name(session.messages)
                error_message = res.get("error")
                break

        succeeded = (
            session.state in (SessionState.IDLE, SessionState.COMPLETED)
            and failed_at is None
        )

        return TurnResult(
            succeeded=succeeded,
            failed_at=failed_at,
            inline_results=inline_results,
            final_state=session.state,
            error_message=error_message,
        )

    # --- Inspection -------------------------------------------------

    @property
    def vfs(self) -> _VFSView:
        """Convenient `harness.vfs[path]` accessor over the live VFS."""
        return _VFSView(self._ensure_session().session_manager.vfs)

    @property
    def messages(self) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
        """The session's message list (live; mutate at your peril)."""
        return self._ensure_session().messages

    @property
    def session(self) -> LiveSession:
        """Escape hatch — direct access to the underlying LiveSession."""
        return self._ensure_session()

    @property
    def session_manager(self) -> SessionManager:
        """Escape hatch — direct access to the underlying SessionManager."""
        self._ensure_session()
        assert self._session_manager is not None
        return self._session_manager

    @property
    def last_turn(self) -> TurnResult:
        """The TurnResult from the most recent run_turn().

        Raises if no turn has run yet (avoids None-checking in tests).
        """
        if self._last_turn is None:
            raise RuntimeError("No turn has run yet — call run_turn() first.")
        return self._last_turn

    @property
    def prompt_blocks(self) -> list[Any]:
        """Direct access to PromptManager.blocks for advanced assertions."""
        return self._ensure_session().session_manager.prompt_manager.blocks

    @property
    def backend(self) -> ScriptedBackend:
        """Direct access to ScriptedBackend (for assert_drained, etc.)."""
        self._ensure_session()
        return self._backend

    def next_prompt_text(self) -> str:
        """Concatenate every user-role text block from to_messages().

        Mirrors the helper that test_prompt_manager_inline_failure used.
        Useful for "is this string in the prompt the AI would see?"
        substring assertions.
        """
        sm = self._ensure_session().session_manager
        msgs = sm.get_prompt_messages()
        parts: list[str] = []
        for msg in msgs:
            if msg.get("role") != "user":
                continue
            content = msg.get("content")
            if isinstance(content, str):
                parts.append(content)
            elif isinstance(content, list):
                for blk in content:
                    if isinstance(blk, dict) and "text" in blk:
                        parts.append(blk["text"])
        return "\n".join(parts)


# --- Internal lenses -------------------------------------------------


class _VFSView:
    """`harness.vfs[path]` syntax sugar over WorkInProgressVFS."""

    def __init__(self, vfs: Any) -> None:
        self._vfs = vfs

    def __getitem__(self, path: str) -> str:
        return self._vfs.read_file(path)

    def __contains__(self, path: str) -> bool:
        return self._vfs.file_exists(path)

    @property
    def pending_changes(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        """Pass-through for tests that want to inspect VFS pending state."""
        return self._vfs.pending_changes


def _extract_failed_tool_name(messages: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> str | None:
    """Best-effort: scan recent messages for the canonical failure marker.

    LiveSession's failure path injects `❌ \`<tool_name>\` failed:` into
    the prompt. If we find one, return the tool name. Otherwise None.
    """
    import re as _re

    pattern = _re.compile(r"❌ `([^`]+)` failed:")
    for msg in reversed(messages):
        content = msg.get("content")
        if isinstance(content, str):
            m = pattern.search(content)
            if m:
                return m.group(1)
    return None


def _rmtree(path: Path) -> None:
    """Recursively remove a directory tree (Path.rmdir requires empty)."""
    import shutil

    shutil.rmtree(path)
</write>

Now migrate the two existing tests. First, the inline-pipeline VFS test — it's a single-turn flow (`<replace> <replace> <run_tests/>`) where run_tests fails:

<write file="tests/test_inline_pipeline_vfs.py">
"""
Tests for the inline-command pipeline's interaction with the VFS.

These tests run the full LiveSession pipeline (via the test harness) and
assert that VFS state is correct after the pipeline finishes — including
when a later command in the pipeline fails.

Migrated from the original hand-rolled-fixture version as the proof-point
for Phase 4 of the test refactor (see TEST_REFACTOR_TODO.md).
"""


def test_edits_persist_in_vfs_when_run_tests_fails(session):
    """
    Real-world flow: <replace a.py> → <replace b.py> → <run_tests/> → narration.

    Both replaces succeed; run_tests runs against the materialized VFS
    state (which includes both edits) and reports failure because the
    Makefile's `test` target exits non-zero.

    The fact that run_tests failed must not erase the prior edits — both
    edits must still be visible through the VFS after the pipeline.
    """
    session.given_files(
        {
            "a.py": "def foo():\n    return 1\n",
            "b.py": "def bar():\n    return 10\n",
        }
    )
    session.given_failing_tests()
    session.given_files_in_context("a.py", "b.py")

    session.user_says("Fix foo to return 2 and bar to return 20, then run tests.")
    session.ai_says(
        """
        @edit a.py
            old:
                def foo():
                    return 1
            new:
                def foo():
                    return 2

        @edit b.py
            old:
                def bar():
                    return 10
            new:
                def bar():
                    return 20

        @run_tests

        Both functions are updated and the tests should now pass; if anything
        fails I'll investigate further.
        """
    )
    session.run_turn()

    # Pipeline failed at run_tests (the trailing prose is non-executable).
    assert session.last_turn.failed_at == "run_tests", (
        f"expected failure at run_tests; got {session.last_turn.failed_at!r}, "
        f"results={session.last_turn.inline_results}"
    )

    # ── The actual claim under test ────────────────────────────────────
    # Both edits must still be visible through the VFS afterwards.
    assert session.vfs["a.py"] == "def foo():\n    return 2\n", (
        "a.py edit was lost from the VFS after run_tests failed"
    )
    assert session.vfs["b.py"] == "def bar():\n    return 20\n", (
        "b.py edit was lost from the VFS after run_tests failed"
    )

    # And specifically in pending_changes (not somehow committed mid-turn).
    assert "a.py" in session.vfs.pending_changes
    assert "b.py" in session.vfs.pending_changes
</write>

Now the prompt-manager-after-failure test. Original drove `PromptManager` directly. Migrated version drives the real `LiveSession` flow and asserts on `next_prompt_text()` and `prompt_blocks`:

<write file="tests/test_prompt_manager_inline_failure.py">
"""
Test what the AI's next prompt looks like after an inline-command pipeline
fails on `<run_tests/>` that came AFTER successful `<replace>` edits.

Drives the full LiveSession pipeline (via test harness) — same scenario as
test_inline_pipeline_vfs but the lens is the rendered prompt the AI would
see on the next turn, not the VFS.

Verifies that:
  1. The new file content appears in the prompt sent to the AI.
  2. The OLD file content does NOT appear anywhere as live (non-tombstone)
     content. The AI must not see the pre-edit version of the file.
  3. The new content appears AFTER the run_tests failure marker, so the
     AI reads it as post-failure state.
  4. Each edited file has exactly one live FILE_CONTENT block.

Migrated from the original PromptManager-direct version as the second
proof-point for the Phase 4 harness.
"""

from forge.prompts.manager import BlockType


# Distinctive markers so substring searches are unambiguous.
A_OLD_MARKER = "FOO_RETURNS_ONE_v_old_marker"
A_NEW_MARKER = "FOO_RETURNS_TWO_v_new_marker"
B_OLD_MARKER = "BAR_RETURNS_TEN_v_old_marker"
B_NEW_MARKER = "BAR_RETURNS_TWENTY_v_new_marker"


def _setup_failed_pipeline(session) -> None:
    """Common setup: edits + failing run_tests; drive one turn."""
    session.given_files(
        {
            "a.py": f"def foo():\n    return 1  # {A_OLD_MARKER}\n",
            "b.py": f"def bar():\n    return 10  # {B_OLD_MARKER}\n",
        }
    )
    session.given_failing_tests()
    session.given_files_in_context("a.py", "b.py")

    session.user_says("Fix foo to return 2 and bar to return 20, then run tests.")
    session.ai_says(
        f"""
        @edit a.py
            old:
                def foo():
                    return 1  # {A_OLD_MARKER}
            new:
                def foo():
                    return 2  # {A_NEW_MARKER}

        @edit b.py
            old:
                def bar():
                    return 10  # {B_OLD_MARKER}
            new:
                def bar():
                    return 20  # {B_NEW_MARKER}

        @run_tests
        """
    )
    session.run_turn()
    assert session.last_turn.failed_at == "run_tests", (
        "test setup invariant broken: pipeline did not fail at run_tests"
    )


def test_new_file_content_present_in_prompt_after_failed_pipeline(session):
    """The AI must see the NEW content of edited files after a failed pipeline."""
    _setup_failed_pipeline(session)

    text = session.next_prompt_text()

    assert A_NEW_MARKER in text, (
        "a.py's NEW content is missing from the prompt sent to the AI. "
        "The AI will think its edits were not applied."
    )
    assert B_NEW_MARKER in text, (
        "b.py's NEW content is missing from the prompt sent to the AI."
    )


def test_old_file_content_not_visible_as_live_content(session):
    """
    The OLD pre-edit content must NOT appear as live content anywhere.

    Tombstone blocks are bare placeholders without the original content;
    if a tombstone or other block still carries the old marker, that's
    a bug.
    """
    _setup_failed_pipeline(session)

    for block in session.prompt_blocks:
        if block.deleted:
            continue
        if block.block_type != BlockType.FILE_CONTENT:
            continue
        if block.metadata.get("tombstone"):
            assert A_OLD_MARKER not in block.content, (
                f"Tombstone for {block.metadata.get('filepath')} still "
                f"contains the OLD content marker — it should be a bare "
                f"placeholder. Tombstone content: {block.content!r}"
            )
            assert B_OLD_MARKER not in block.content, (
                f"Tombstone for {block.metadata.get('filepath')} still "
                f"contains the OLD content marker."
            )
            continue
        assert A_OLD_MARKER not in block.content, (
            f"File block for {block.metadata.get('filepath')} still "
            f"contains the OLD content for a.py — duplicate stale block."
        )
        assert B_OLD_MARKER not in block.content, (
            f"File block for {block.metadata.get('filepath')} still "
            f"contains the OLD content for b.py — duplicate stale block."
        )

    text = session.next_prompt_text()
    assert A_OLD_MARKER not in text, (
        "a.py's OLD content (pre-edit) is still present in the prompt "
        "sent to the AI. The AI will see two versions and may believe "
        "the edit didn't take effect."
    )
    assert B_OLD_MARKER not in text, (
        "b.py's OLD content (pre-edit) is still present in the prompt."
    )


def test_each_edited_file_appears_exactly_once_as_live_content(session):
    """Each edited file should have exactly one live FILE_CONTENT block."""
    _setup_failed_pipeline(session)

    live_blocks_by_file: dict[str, list] = {}
    for block in session.prompt_blocks:
        if block.deleted:
            continue
        if block.block_type != BlockType.FILE_CONTENT:
            continue
        if block.metadata.get("tombstone"):
            continue
        fp = block.metadata.get("filepath")
        live_blocks_by_file.setdefault(fp, []).append(block)

    assert "a.py" in live_blocks_by_file, "a.py has no live file block"
    assert "b.py" in live_blocks_by_file, "b.py has no live file block"

    assert len(live_blocks_by_file["a.py"]) == 1, (
        f"a.py has {len(live_blocks_by_file['a.py'])} live file blocks, "
        f"expected exactly 1"
    )
    assert len(live_blocks_by_file["b.py"]) == 1, (
        f"b.py has {len(live_blocks_by_file['b.py'])} live file blocks, "
        f"expected exactly 1"
    )

    assert A_NEW_MARKER in live_blocks_by_file["a.py"][0].content
    assert B_NEW_MARKER in live_blocks_by_file["b.py"][0].content


def test_new_content_appears_after_run_tests_error_in_prompt(session):
    """
    The new file content must appear AFTER the run_tests failure marker
    in the rendered prompt — otherwise the AI may interpret the new
    content as the pre-failure state.
    """
    _setup_failed_pipeline(session)

    text = session.next_prompt_text()
    failure_pos = text.find("run_tests")
    a_new_pos = text.find(A_NEW_MARKER)
    b_new_pos = text.find(B_NEW_MARKER)

    assert failure_pos >= 0, "run_tests failure marker missing from prompt"
    assert a_new_pos >= 0, "A_NEW marker missing from prompt"
    assert b_new_pos >= 0, "B_NEW marker missing from prompt"

    assert a_new_pos > failure_pos, (
        "a.py's new content appears BEFORE the run_tests failure message "
        "in the prompt. The AI may read it as pre-failure state."
    )
    assert b_new_pos > failure_pos, (
        "b.py's new content appears BEFORE the run_tests failure message."
    )
</write>

Need to verify this hypothesis and fix (probably: have ScriptedBackend's stream() yield a "no more responses" sentinel or have the harness configure max-turn-recursion behavior).

**Design decisions made for the harness:**
- `user_says()` queues, doesn't trigger
- `ai_says()` queues a scripted response (DSL→inline XML compilation)
- `run_turn()` is the explicit trigger; sends queued user message and drains synchronously
- `TurnResult` dataclass: succeeded, failed_at, inline_results, final_state, error_message
- `harness.vfs[path]` accessor via `_VFSView` wrapper class
- `harness.next_prompt_text()` returns concat of user-role text from `to_messages()`
- `harness.prompt_blocks` for direct PromptManager block inspection
- Lazy session construction (deferred until first use, so `given_files` is cheap)
- `start_summary_generation` monkeypatched to no-op in `__init__` BEFORE constructing SessionManager
- Settings built via `Settings.__new__(Settings); s.config = {}` (matches existing test pattern)

**DSL syntax:**
- `@edit <path>` with indented `old:` / `new:` sub-blocks
- `@write <path>` with indented body
- `@delete <path>`
- `@rename old -> new`
- `@run_tests` (optional attrs in head)
- `@check`
- `@commit message=<text>` or just `@commit`
- `@think` with body becomes `<conclusion>...</conclusion>`
- Indentation-significant body parsing
- Anything not starting with `@` passes through as prose
- `_DIRECTIVE_RE = re.compile(r"^(\s*)@(\w+)(.*)$")`
- `_split_labeled_sections` helper for `old:` / `new:` parsing inside `@edit` body

**Repo helper (`tests/harness/repo.py`):**
- `bootstrap_repo(tmp_path, files=None, branch="master")` returns ForgeRepository with one initial commit on master containing `files`. Defaults to a `.gitkeep` if no files given.
- `PASSING_MAKEFILE = "test:\\n\\t@printf 'simulated test pass\\\\n'; true\\n"`
- `FAILING_MAKEFILE = "test:\\n\\t@printf 'simulated test failure\\\\n'; false\\n"`
- Uses `pygit2.Signature("Test", "test@test.com")` as default sig.

**Action plan from here (in priority order):**
1. **Critical — fix the footgun first:** rewrite the broken `tests/harness/dsl.py` using a `<write_NONCE>` nonced block, since the body legitimately contains literal `<replace>`, `<old>`, `<new>`, `<write>`, `<delete>`, `<rename>`, `<run_tests/>`, `<check/>`, `<commit>`, `<conclusion>` strings. Pick a nonce like `dsl1` that doesn't appear in the body.
2. **Verify the recursion hypothesis** about ScriptedBackend exhausted → infinite retry. Look at `LiveSession._on_stream_error` → `_process_llm_request()`. Either:
   - (a) ScriptedBackend should NOT raise on exhaustion — should yield StreamFinished(content=None, tool_calls=None) so the session naturally finishes the turn. This is friendlier for tests.
   - (b) `_on_stream_error` should NOT auto-retry indefinitely. Was it always this way? Yes — pre-Phase-2 code did the same retry-on-error.
   - Best fix: option (a). Change ScriptedBackend.stream() to yield a finished marker when queue is empty rather than raising. (Or expose a strict-mode flag.)
3. Recreate `tests/conftest.py`, `tests/harness/session.py`, the two migrated tests, and the TODO update.
4. Run tests + commit.

Note: forge/session/manager.py, forge/session/live_session.py, tests/test_branch_workspace_vfs.py, forge/tools/invocation.py were unloaded.

Files needed back in context for the fix: forge/session/live_session.py (to verify _on_stream_error logic), forge/runtime/llm_backend.py (to fix ScriptedBackend exhaustion behavior)."}

{"content": ">>>    1 | """
       2 | DSL compiler: terse test-friendly syntax → inline XML the parser understands.
       3 | 
       4 | Why a DSL? Because writing inline-command XML directly inside a Python
       5 | test means literal `<replace>`/`</old>`/`</new>` tags inside triple-quoted
       6 | strings, which is awkward to read AND occasionally trips up tooling that
       7 | scans the source. The DSL keeps tests skimmable.
       8 | 
       9 | Supported directives (everything else passes through as prose):
      10 | 
      11 |     @edit <path>
      12 |         old: <text...>
      13 |         new: <text...>
      14 | 
      15 |     @write <path>
      16 |         <full file content>
      17 | 
      18 |     @delete <path>
      19 | 
      20 |     @rename <old_path> -> <new_path>
      21 | 
      22 |     @run_tests
      23 |     @check
      24 |     @commit message=<text>
      25 |     @think
      26 |         <conclusion text>
      27 | 
      28 | Indentation rules (whitespace-significant, like YAML or Python):
      29 | 
      30 |   - A directive line starts with `@` at any column.
      31 |   - Subsequent lines that are MORE INDENTED than the directive are part
      32 |     of the directive's body. Once we see a line that is at-or-less-
      33 |     indented than the directive (and isn't blank), the body ends.
      34 |   - Inside an `@edit` body, the labels `old:` and `new:` start their
      35 |     respective sections; their content runs until the next label, the
      36 |     next `@` directive, or end-of-body.
      37 |   - Blank lines are kept as blank lines; they do NOT end a body.
      38 | 
      39 | Anything outside a directive is emitted verbatim as prose, so you can
      40 | mix narration with commands the way an LLM actually would.
      41 | 
      42 | Escape hatch: write inline XML directly. The compiler only consumes
      43 | lines beginning with `@` (after optional leading whitespace) — raw XML
      44 | is just prose to it.
      45 | """
      46 | 
      47 | from __future__ import annotations
      48 | 
      49 | import re
      50 | import textwrap
      51 | from dataclasses import dataclass
      52 | 
      53 | 
      54 | @dataclass
      55 | class _Directive:
      56 |     name: str  # e.g. "edit", "run_tests"
      57 |     head: str  # text on the directive line after the name (e.g. "a.py")
      58 |     body_lines: list[str]  # de-indented body lines (no trailing newline each)
      59 |     indent: int  # column where the `@` started
      60 | 
      61 | 
      62 | def compile_dsl(text: str) -> str:
      63 |     """Compile DSL `text` into the assistant content format (prose + inline XML).
      64 | 
      65 |     Pure function. The output is what an LLM "would have said" — you can
      66 |     feed it straight into ScriptedBackend.queue_response(content=...) or
      67 |     pass it through parse_inline_commands directly.
      68 |     """
      69 |     text = textwrap.dedent(text).strip("\n")
      70 |     lines = text.split("\n")
      71 | 
      72 |     out_parts: list[str] = []
      73 |     i = 0
      74 |     while i < len(lines):
      75 |         line = lines[i]
      76 |         stripped = line.lstrip()
      77 | 
      78 |         if stripped.startswith("@"):
      79 |             directive, consumed = _parse_directive(lines, i)
      80 |             out_parts.append(_render_directive(directive))
      81 |             i += consumed
      82 |         else:
      83 |             # Plain prose line — emit verbatim.
      84 |             out_parts.append(line)
      85 |             i += 1
      86 | 
      87 |     # Strip leading/trailing blank lines from the joined output but keep
      88 |     # internal structure.
      89 |     return "\n".join(out_parts).strip("\n") + "\n"
      90 | 
      91 | 
      92 | # --- Parsing ---------------------------------------------------------
      93 | 
      94 | 
      95 | _DIRECTIVE_RE = re.compile(r"^(\s*)@(\w+)(.*)$")
      96 | 
      97 | 
      98 | def _parse_directive(lines: list[str], start: int) -> tuple[_Directive, int]:
      99 |     """Parse a directive at lines[start]. Returns (directive, lines_consumed)."""
     100 |     m = _DIRECTIVE_RE.match(lines[start])
     101 |     if m is None:  # pragma: no cover — caller already checked
     102 |         raise ValueError(f"not a directive: {lines[start]!r}")
     103 |     indent_str, name, head = m.groups()
     104 |     indent = len(indent_str)
     105 |     head = head.strip()
     106 | 
     107 |     # Collect body lines: those more indented than the directive (or blank).
     108 |     body_lines: list[str] = []
     109 |     j = start + 1
     110 |     while j < len(lines):
     111 |         candidate = lines[j]
     112 |         if candidate.strip() == "":
     113 |             body_lines.append("")
     114 |             j += 1
     115 |             continue
     116 |         # Stop if we're back at-or-less indented than the directive.
     117 |         leading = len(candidate) - len(candidate.lstrip())
     118 |         if leading <= indent:
     119 |             break
     120 |         body_lines.append(candidate)
     121 |         j += 1
     122 | 
     123 |     # De-indent the body to the minimum indent of non-blank lines.
     124 |     if body_lines:
     125 |         body_lines = _dedent_block(body_lines)
     126 | 
     127 |     return _Directive(name=name, head=head, body_lines=body_lines, indent=indent), j - start
     128 | 
     129 | 
     130 | def _dedent_block(lines: list[str]) -> list[str]:
     131 |     """De-indent a block to its smallest non-blank indent."""
     132 |     min_indent = min(
     133 |         (len(line) - len(line.lstrip()) for line in lines if line.strip()),
     134 |         default=0,
     135 |     )
     136 |     return [line[min_indent:] if line.strip() else "" for line in lines]
     137 | 
     138 | 
     139 | # --- Rendering -------------------------------------------------------
     140 | 
     141 | 
     142 | def _render_directive(d: _Directive) -> str:
     143 |     handler = _RENDERERS.get(d.name)
     144 |     if handler is None:
     145 |         raise ValueError(
     146 |             f"unknown DSL directive: @{d.name}. "
     147 |             f"Known: {sorted(_RENDERERS)}. "
     148 |             f"For unsupported tools, write inline XML directly."
     149 |         )
     150 |     return handler(d)
     151 | 
     152 | 
     153 | def _render_edit(d: _Directive) -> str:
     154 |     """@edit <path> with `old:` / `new:` sub-blocks."""
     155 |     path = d.head.strip()
     156 |     if not path:
     157 |         raise ValueError("@edit requires a file path")
     158 | 
     159 |     sections = _split_labeled_sections(d.body_lines, ("old:", "new:"))
     160 |     if "old" not in sections or "new" not in sections:
     161 |         raise ValueError(
     162 |             f"@edit {path}: requires both `old:` and `new:` sub-blocks; "
     163 |             f"got {sorted(sections)}"
     164 |         )
     165 | 
     166 |     old_text = "\n".join(sections["old"]).rstrip("\n")
     167 |     new_text = "\n".join(sections["new"]).rstrip("\n")
     168 | 
     169 |     return (
     170 |         f'<replace file="{path}">\n'
     171 |         f"<old>\n{old_text}\n</old>\n"
     172 |         f"<new>\n{new_text}\n</new>\n"
     173 |         f"</replace>"
     174 |     )
     175 | 
     176 | 
     177 | def _render_write(d: _Directive) -> str:
     178 |     """@write <path> with raw body."""
     179 |     path = d.head.strip()
     180 |     if not path:
     181 |         raise ValueError("@write requires a file path")
     182 |     body = "\n".join(d.body_lines).rstrip("\n")
     183 |     return f'<write file="{path}">\n{body}\n", "filepath": "tests/harness/dsl.py", "range": "1-183", "side_effects": ["ephemeral_result"], "success": true, "target_line": 1, "total_lines": 183}

{"content": ">>>    1 | """
       2 | Git repository bootstrap helpers for the test harness.
       3 | 
       4 | Extracted from the duplicated fixtures in tests/test_branch_workspace_vfs.py
       5 | and tests/test_inline_pipeline_vfs.py. Both did the same dance: init a
       6 | pygit2 repo, write a tree of seed files, create one commit on master.
       7 | """
       8 | 
       9 | from pathlib import Path
      10 | 
      11 | import pygit2
      12 | 
      13 | from forge.git_backend.repository import ForgeRepository
      14 | 
      15 | _DEFAULT_SIG = pygit2.Signature("Test", "test@test.com")
      16 | 
      17 | 
      18 | def bootstrap_repo(
      19 |     tmp_path: Path,
      20 |     files: dict[str, str | bytes] | None = None,
      21 |     branch: str = "master",
      22 | ) -> ForgeRepository:
      23 |     """Initialize a git repo at tmp_path with one commit holding `files`.
      24 | 
      25 |     Args:
      26 |         tmp_path: directory to init the repo in (typically the pytest tmp_path).
      27 |         files: path -> content mapping for the seed commit. Empty/None creates
      28 |             a repo with a single placeholder file (`.gitkeep`) so the initial
      29 |             commit isn't empty (some tooling chokes on empty trees).
      30 |         branch: branch name to create the commit on. Defaults to "master" to
      31 |             match the existing test suite's convention.
      32 | 
      33 |     Returns:
      34 |         A ForgeRepository pointing at the new repo, with HEAD on `branch`.
      35 |     """
      36 |     pygit2.init_repository(str(tmp_path))
      37 |     raw = pygit2.Repository(str(tmp_path))
      38 | 
      39 |     if not files:
      40 |         files = {".gitkeep": ""}
      41 | 
      42 |     tb = raw.TreeBuilder()
      43 |     for name, data in files.items():
      44 |         if isinstance(data, str):
      45 |             data = data.encode()
      46 |         blob = raw.create_blob(data)
      47 |         tb.insert(name, blob, pygit2.GIT_FILEMODE_BLOB)
      48 |     tree = tb.write()
      49 |     raw.create_commit(
      50 |         f"refs/heads/{branch}", _DEFAULT_SIG, _DEFAULT_SIG, "initial", tree, []
      51 |     )
      52 |     raw.set_head(f"refs/heads/{branch}")
      53 | 
      54 |     return ForgeRepository(str(tmp_path))
      55 | 
      56 | 
      57 | # Hermetic Makefile bodies for given_passing_tests / given_failing_tests.
      58 | # Both use printf + true/false so they don't depend on bash being available
      59 | # and don't touch any files (writeback after the test command is therefore
      60 | # always a no-op, which is what we want for deterministic flow tests).
      61 | PASSING_MAKEFILE = "test:\n\t@printf 'simulated test pass\\n'; true\n"

<write_dsl1 file="tests/harness/dsl.py">
"""
DSL compiler: terse test-friendly syntax to inline XML the parser understands.

Why a DSL? Because writing inline-command XML directly inside a Python
test means literal replace/old/new tags inside triple-quoted strings,
which is awkward to read. The DSL keeps tests skimmable.

Supported directives (everything else passes through as prose):

    @edit <path>
        old: <text...>
        new: <text...>

    @write <path>
        <full file content>

    @delete <path>

    @rename <old_path> -> <new_path>

    @run_tests
    @check
    @commit message=<text>
    @think
        <conclusion text>

Indentation rules (whitespace-significant, like YAML or Python):

  - A directive line starts with @ at any column.
  - Subsequent lines that are MORE INDENTED than the directive are part
    of the directive's body. Once we see a line that is at-or-less-
    indented than the directive (and isn't blank), the body ends.
  - Inside an @edit body, the labels old: and new: start their
    respective sections; their content runs until the next label, the
    next @ directive, or end-of-body.
  - Blank lines are kept as blank lines; they do NOT end a body.

Anything outside a directive is emitted verbatim as prose, so you can
mix narration with commands the way an LLM actually would.

Escape hatch: write inline XML directly. The compiler only consumes
lines beginning with @ (after optional leading whitespace) — raw XML
is just prose to it.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import re
import textwrap
from collections.abc import Callable
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class _Directive:
    name: str  # e.g. "edit", "run_tests"
    head: str  # text on the directive line after the name (e.g. "a.py")
    body_lines: list[str]  # de-indented body lines (no trailing newline each)
    indent: int  # column where the @ started


def compile_dsl(text: str) -> str:
    """Compile DSL `text` into the assistant content format (prose + inline XML).

    Pure function. The output is what an LLM "would have said" — you can
    feed it straight into ScriptedBackend.queue_response(content=...) or
    pass it through parse_inline_commands directly.
    """
    text = textwrap.dedent(text).strip("\n")
    lines = text.split("\n")

    out_parts: list[str] = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]
        stripped = line.lstrip()

        if stripped.startswith("@"):
            directive, consumed = _parse_directive(lines, i)
            out_parts.append(_render_directive(directive))
            i += consumed
        else:
            # Plain prose line — emit verbatim.
            out_parts.append(line)
            i += 1

    # Strip leading/trailing blank lines from the joined output but keep
    # internal structure.
    return "\n".join(out_parts).strip("\n") + "\n"


# --- Parsing ---------------------------------------------------------


_DIRECTIVE_RE = re.compile(r"^(\s*)@(\w+)(.*)$")


def _parse_directive(lines: list[str], start: int) -> tuple[_Directive, int]:
    """Parse a directive at lines[start]. Returns (directive, lines_consumed)."""
    m = _DIRECTIVE_RE.match(lines[start])
    if m is None:  # pragma: no cover — caller already checked
        raise ValueError(f"not a directive: {lines[start]!r}")
    indent_str, name, head = m.groups()
    indent = len(indent_str)
    head = head.strip()

    # Collect body lines: those more indented than the directive (or blank).
    body_lines: list[str] = []
    j = start + 1
    while j < len(lines):
        candidate = lines[j]
        if candidate.strip() == "":
            body_lines.append("")
            j += 1
            continue
        # Stop if we're back at-or-less indented than the directive.
        leading = len(candidate) - len(candidate.lstrip())
        if leading <= indent:
            break
        body_lines.append(candidate)
        j += 1

    # Trim trailing blank lines from the body (they're between directives,
    # not part of this body).
    while body_lines and body_lines[-1] == "":
        body_lines.pop()

    # De-indent the body to the minimum indent of non-blank lines.
    if body_lines:
        body_lines = _dedent_block(body_lines)

    return _Directive(name=name, head=head, body_lines=body_lines, indent=indent), j - start


def _dedent_block(lines: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """De-indent a block to its smallest non-blank indent."""
    min_indent = min(
        (len(line) - len(line.lstrip()) for line in lines if line.strip()),
        default=0,
    )
    return [line[min_indent:] if line.strip() else "" for line in lines]


def _split_labeled_sections(
    body_lines: list[str], labels: tuple[str, ...]
) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    """Split a body into named sections introduced by `<label>:` lines.

    A label line looks like `old:` or `new:` (matching one of `labels`,
    case-sensitive, optional trailing inline content on the same line is
    appended as the first body line of that section).

    Lines before the first label are dropped. Sections continue until
    the next label or end-of-body.

    Returns a dict mapping label-without-colon to its dedented body lines.
    """
    sections: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
    current: str | None = None
    current_lines: list[str] = []

    label_set = set(labels)

    for line in body_lines:
        stripped = line.strip()
        matched_label: str | None = None
        for lbl in label_set:
            # A label line is one whose stripped form starts with `lbl`
            # and where the next char is space or end-of-string.
            if stripped == lbl or stripped.startswith(lbl + " ") or stripped.startswith(lbl):
                # Be strict: must be "<label>:" with optional content after.
                if stripped[: len(lbl)] == lbl and (
                    len(stripped) == len(lbl) or stripped[len(lbl)] == " "
                ):
                    matched_label = lbl
                    break

        if matched_label is not None:
            # Close out previous section.
            if current is not None:
                sections[current] = _dedent_block(current_lines)
            current = matched_label.rstrip(":")
            current_lines = []
            inline_after = stripped[len(matched_label) :].lstrip()
            if inline_after:
                current_lines.append(inline_after)
        elif current is not None:
            current_lines.append(line)
        # else: line before first label — ignored.

    if current is not None:
        sections[current] = _dedent_block(current_lines)

    return sections


# --- Rendering -------------------------------------------------------


def _render_directive(d: _Directive) -> str:
    handler = _RENDERERS.get(d.name)
    if handler is None:
        raise ValueError(
            f"unknown DSL directive: @{d.name}. "
            f"Known: {sorted(_RENDERERS)}. "
            f"For unsupported tools, write inline XML directly."
        )
    return handler(d)


_LT = "<"
_GT = ">"
_SLASH = "/"


def _open_tag(name: str, attrs: str = "") -> str:
    return f"{_LT}{name}{(' ' + attrs) if attrs else ''}{_GT}"


def _close_tag(name: str) -> str:
    return f"{_LT}{_SLASH}{name}{_GT}"


def _self_closing(name: str, attrs: str = "") -> str:
    return f"{_LT}{name}{(' ' + attrs) if attrs else ''}{_SLASH}{_GT}"


def _render_edit(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@edit <path> with old:/new: sub-blocks."""
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@edit requires a file path")

    sections = _split_labeled_sections(d.body_lines, ("old:", "new:"))
    if "old" not in sections or "new" not in sections:
        raise ValueError(
            f"@edit {path}: requires both old: and new: sub-blocks; "
            f"got {sorted(sections)}"
        )

    old_text = "\n".join(sections["old"]).rstrip("\n")
    new_text = "\n".join(sections["new"]).rstrip("\n")

    open_replace = _open_tag("replace", f'file="{path}"')
    close_replace = _close_tag("replace")
    open_old = _open_tag("old")
    close_old = _close_tag("old")
    open_new = _open_tag("new")
    close_new = _close_tag("new")

    return (
        f"{open_replace}\n"
        f"{open_old}\n{old_text}\n{close_old}\n"
        f"{open_new}\n{new_text}\n{close_new}\n"
        f"{close_replace}"
    )


def _render_write(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@write <path> with raw body."""
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@write requires a file path")
    body = "\n".join(d.body_lines).rstrip("\n")
    open_w = _open_tag("write", f'file="{path}"')
    close_w = _close_tag("write")
    return f"{open_w}\n{body}\n{close_w}"


def _render_delete(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@delete <path>."""
    path = d.head.strip()
    if not path:
        raise ValueError("@delete requires a file path")
    return _self_closing("delete", f'file="{path}"')


def _render_rename(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@rename <old> -> <new>."""
    head = d.head.strip()
    if "->" not in head:
        raise ValueError(f"@rename requires `<old> -> <new>` syntax; got {head!r}")
    old, new = (p.strip() for p in head.split("->", 1))
    if not old or not new:
        raise ValueError(f"@rename: both old and new paths required; got {head!r}")
    return _self_closing("rename", f'from="{old}" to="{new}"')


def _render_run_tests(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@run_tests with optional head attrs (passed through verbatim)."""
    attrs = d.head.strip()
    return _self_closing("run_tests", attrs)


def _render_check(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@check."""
    attrs = d.head.strip()
    return _self_closing("check", attrs)


def _render_commit(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@commit [message=<text>]."""
    head = d.head.strip()
    # Parse `message=...` if present. Quote it for safety.
    msg = ""
    m = re.match(r'message\s*=\s*(?:"([^"]*)"|(\S.*))$', head)
    if m:
        msg = m.group(1) if m.group(1) is not None else m.group(2)
    elif head:
        msg = head  # bare text after @commit becomes the message
    attrs = f'message="{msg}"' if msg else ""
    return _self_closing("commit", attrs)


def _render_think(d: _Directive) -> str:
    """@think with body becomes a <conclusion>...</conclusion> block.

    The think tool reads <conclusion> from the assistant text. The body
    of @think is passed verbatim as the conclusion text.
    """
    body = "\n".join(d.body_lines).rstrip("\n")
    open_c = _open_tag("conclusion")
    close_c = _close_tag("conclusion")
    return f"{open_c}\n{body}\n{close_c}"


_RENDERERS: dict[str, Callable[[_Directive], str]] = {
    "edit": _render_edit,
    "write": _render_write,
    "delete": _render_delete,
    "rename": _render_rename,
    "run_tests": _render_run_tests,
    "check": _render_check,
    "commit": _render_commit,
    "think": _render_think,
}


__all__ = ["compile_dsl"]
